content.text: Providing a language model with one or more labeled input-output pairs before the live task is one of the fastest ways to steer its behavior — no retraining required.

exploits the transformer's in-context learning ability: the model reads the demonstrated pattern and generalises it to new inputs within the same inference call. It sits between zero-shot instruction (no examples) and fine-tuning (weight updates), combining zero-shot flexibility with dramatically better format and style control.

— one or more (input, output) pairs — is prepended to the prompt before the live query. The model never sees labeled data during pretraining for this specific task; instead, the examples supplied at inference time provide an implicit spec of the desired output format, reasoning depth, label vocabulary, and edge-case handling.

The technique was popularised in the GPT-3 paper (Brown et al., 2020), which showed that a 175-billion-parameter model could perform competitive classification, translation, and question-answering with as few as one example — without any gradient update. The term
is used when exactly one example is given;
typically means two to eight; and
refers to dozens or hundreds of examples filling the available context window.
Each demonstration usually follows a consistent template: an input field (optionally labelled with a delimiter such as

or XML tags), an optional reasoning trace (used in chain-of-thought variants), and a clearly delineated output. The live query repeats the input portion of the template without the output; the model completes it.

Language models pre-trained on web-scale corpora develop powerful but generic capabilities. Deployed agents operate in narrower domains — customer support systems, financial data extractors, medical summarisers — where idiosyncratic output formats, terminology, and decision boundaries are non-negotiable.

Zero-shot instructions often fail to convey subtleties that a single concrete example communicates instantly: whether the output should omit punctuation, how to handle ambiguous edge cases, whether to quote the input in the response. Few-shot examples encode these implicit rules more compactly than long prose instruction blocks, and they sidestep the interpretation errors that arise when a model must infer format from description alone.

For agentic systems — where output quality feeds directly into downstream tool calls, branching decisions, or structured data stores — even modest format deviations can cascade into hard-to-debug failures. Few-shot examples act as a
between the prompt author and the model, enforced every time the prompt is submitted.
— The model mimics the demonstrated structure character-for-character. JSON field order, date formats, indentation, and delimiter choices are all reliably reproduced.
— Edge cases shown by example do not need to be enumerated in prose; the model infers the rule from the instance.
— Updating behavior requires editing the example block in the prompt, not collecting datasets, running fine-tunes, or deploying new model weights.
— Different customers or locales can receive different example sets from the same base model without infrastructure changes.
— Few-shot examples work on top of system instructions, not instead of them; the two reinforce each other.
— The technique applies uniformly across GPT, Claude, Llama, Gemini, and every other transformer-based LLM that supports a prompt string or message list.
— Each example consumes prompt tokens. With long examples and 4–8 shots, token cost at inference time can double or triple compared with zero-shot.
— In agents with long retrieval payloads or multi-turn history, large example blocks crowd out information the model needs for the actual task.
— Incorrect or inconsistent examples teach the wrong behavior just as reliably as correct ones. A single malformed demonstration can dominate the model's response pattern.
— The sequence of examples affects outputs; the last example before the query tends to have disproportionate influence.
— Static example sets work for homogeneous inputs; heterogeneous inputs require retrieval-based dynamic selection, which adds system complexity.
— If the task or output schema evolves, example blocks must be updated manually or via automated pipelines; neglecting this creates silent regressions.

Transformers process input as a flat sequence of tokens. During the forward pass, every token attends to every earlier token (in causal / decoder-only architectures). The demonstration pairs — treated as ordinary tokens — become part of the model's effective input distribution and shape the probability estimates for future tokens.
Formally, let the prompt consist of
examples followed by a live query:

No gradient update occurs. The conditioning is purely through the attention mechanism: the residual stream at the position of the live query's first output token integrates information from all prior example tokens via cross-position attention. The demonstrations shift the implicit prior over output tokens without changing any model parameters — this is the
(ICL) effect studied since the GPT-3 paper.

Research has shown that the labels in few-shot examples matter less than their format and distribution when the model is large enough. Small models rely more on the label accuracy of examples; large models primarily extract structural patterns. Practically,
— both for reliability across model sizes and for auditability.

The effective information per example follows a diminishing-returns curve. Empirically, moving from zero to one shot provides the largest quality gain; the marginal gain from example N+1 decreases rapidly. Beyond eight examples the gains typically fall below noise, while token cost continues to grow linearly.

The diagram below shows how a few-shot prompt is structured and how the model consumes it. The prompt is a flat sequence; the shaded boxes are logical groupings visible to the prompt author but transparent to the model.

attrs.alt: Few-Shot Prompting Architecture: demonstration examples, live query, and model in-context learning flow

content.text: Key observations from the diagram: examples and the live query share the same token stream — there is no architectural boundary between them. The LLM's attention mechanism (shown as the arrow labeled
) reads across all example tokens when generating the output. The
label inside the LLM box is critical: few-shot prompting is entirely an inference-time technique.
Zero-shot prompts fail to hit evaluation thresholds and the task has a consistent input-output pattern that can be demonstrated.
Output format is idiosyncratic (non-standard JSON shapes, custom delimiters, domain-specific shorthand).
Subtle classification or extraction boundaries need illustration that prose instructions fail to convey.
Rapid iteration is more valuable than a training pipeline — new domains or clients can be served by swapping the example block.
The model must handle organization-specific conventions (date formats, product codes, abbreviation vocabularies).
The context window is already heavily used by retrieval payloads, tool results, or conversation history — examples will crowd out essential information.
Native schema enforcement (e.g., OpenAI function calling with JSON Schema, Anthropic tool_use) already guarantees the needed structure; examples are redundant.
The task varies so widely across calls that a static example set would mislead more often than help; in that case, use dynamic few-shot retrieval or zero-shot.
Sensitive domains where incorrect training examples could introduce harmful or biased patterns.
Latency or token-cost budgets are extremely tight and every token counts.
Implementation follows four steps: build an example library, select and order examples, format the prompt, and evaluate. Each is discussed below.

Collect real inputs and their verified correct outputs from production logs, domain experts, or annotated datasets. Aim for diversity: at least one example per major input class, and examples that represent edge cases. Store examples as structured records so they can be retrieved, filtered, and versioned.

For a static set, choose 3–5 examples covering distinct output patterns. For a dynamic set, embed the live query with a text embedding model and retrieve the top-K nearest examples from the library using cosine similarity. Place examples from least to most similar to the live query; models tend to weight examples near the query boundary more heavily.
Use a consistent, unambiguous delimiter. XML-style tags (

) or Markdown fences both work. The critical rule is that the same delimiter structure must be used in both examples and the live query so the model can identify the pattern boundary. Clearly mark where the model should begin generating.

Run the prompt against a held-out evaluation set. Track accuracy, format compliance, and latency. When accuracy plateaus, inspect failure cases to determine whether a new example covering that pattern would help, or whether a different technique (chain-of-thought, fine-tuning) is required.
The following script sends a three-shot prompt to classify product review sentiment. It demonstrates the minimal viable structure: a system instruction, three example pairs, and the live query.

"""
Simple few-shot sentiment classifier.
Run: pip install anthropic && python sentiment_classifier.py
"""
import anthropic, json

SYSTEM = "You are a sentiment classifier. Output exactly one word: Positive, Negative, or Neutral."

SHOTS = [
    ("This product exceeded all my expectations!", "Positive"),
    ("Arrived broken and customer service was unhelpful.", "Negative"),
    ("It's an ordinary coffee mug. Does what it says.", "Neutral"),
]

def classify(review: str) -> str:
    # Build the few-shot message history using alternating turns
    messages = []
    for user_text, assistant_text in SHOTS:
        messages.append({"role": "user", "content": user_text})
        messages.append({"role": "assistant", "content": assistant_text})
    # Append the live query
    messages.append({"role": "user", "content": review})

    client = anthropic.Anthropic()
    resp = client.messages.create(
        model="claude-haiku-4-5",
        max_tokens=8,
        system=SYSTEM,
        messages=messages,
    )
    return resp.content[0].text.strip()

reviews = [
    "Honestly the best headphones I have ever owned.",
    "Stopped working after two weeks. Very disappointed.",
    "It connects to Bluetooth. Nothing special.",
]
for r in reviews:
    print(f"{classify(r):<10} | {r}")
Example 2 — Dynamic few-shot with semantic retrieval (Python)

This more realistic example retrieves the top-K most similar examples from an in-memory vector store before calling the model. In production, replace the toy embedding with a real embedding API and the list with a vector database.

"""
Dynamic few-shot: retrieve semantically similar examples before each call.
Requires: pip install anthropic numpy
"""
import json, anthropic
import numpy as np

# --- Minimal cosine similarity retrieval ---
def cosine_sim(a, b):
    return float(np.dot(a, b) / (np.linalg.norm(a) * np.linalg.norm(b) + 1e-9))

def embed_toy(text: str) -> np.ndarray:
    """Toy bag-of-chars embedding; replace with a real API in production."""
    vec = np.zeros(128)
    for i, ch in enumerate(text.lower()):
        vec[ord(ch) % 128] += 1
    norm = np.linalg.norm(vec)
    return vec / norm if norm > 0 else vec

# Example library — each entry has input, output, and its embedding
LIBRARY = [
    {"input": "What is the capital of France?",     "output": "Paris"},
    {"input": "What is the capital of Germany?",    "output": "Berlin"},
    {"input": "What is the capital of Japan?",      "output": "Tokyo"},
    {"input": "Who wrote Hamlet?",                  "output": "William Shakespeare"},
    {"input": "Who wrote 1984?",                    "output": "George Orwell"},
    {"input": "Who painted the Mona Lisa?",         "output": "Leonardo da Vinci"},
]
for ex in LIBRARY:
    ex["emb"] = embed_toy(ex["input"])

def retrieve(query: str, k: int = 3) -> list[dict]:
    q_emb = embed_toy(query)
    scored = sorted(LIBRARY, key=lambda e: cosine_sim(e["emb"], q_emb), reverse=True)
    return scored[:k]

def answer(question: str) -> str:
    examples = retrieve(question, k=3)
    # Build prompt string
    shots = "\n".join(
        f"Q: {e['input']}\nA: {e['output']}" for e in examples
    )
    prompt = f"{shots}\nQ: {question}\nA:"

    client = anthropic.Anthropic()
    resp = client.messages.create(
        model="claude-haiku-4-5",
        max_tokens=32,
        messages=[{"role": "user", "content": prompt}],
    )
    return resp.content[0].text.strip()

print(answer("What is the capital of Italy?"))   # Rome
print(answer("Who wrote Animal Farm?"))           # George Orwell

A B2B SaaS company operates a customer-support agent that must route incoming tickets to one of 14 internal queues (billing, technical, onboarding, security, ...). The routing model is called on every ticket — tens of thousands per day — so latency and token cost matter.

The team's initial zero-shot system prompt achieved 71% routing accuracy on a held-out evaluation set. Adding five static few-shot examples (one per the five most ambiguous queue boundaries) raised accuracy to 84% within a day. Adding dynamic few-shot retrieval — embedding each ticket at runtime and selecting the five most similar resolved tickets from a 2,000-example vector store — raised accuracy to 91%, with no model training.
The production system embeds the ticket with a lightweight embedding model (under 5 ms), retrieves five examples from a

table using cosine distance, and prepends them to the routing prompt. Total overhead is under 20 ms per request. The example library is updated nightly from freshly resolved tickets, keeping it aligned with evolving product terminology without any retraining.

# Simplified production-style routing prompt builder
import psycopg2, numpy as np

def build_routing_prompt(ticket_text: str, ticket_embedding: list[float]) -> str:
    conn = psycopg2.connect("postgresql://...")
    cur = conn.cursor()
    # Retrieve nearest 5 resolved tickets using pgvector cosine distance
    cur.execute("""
        SELECT ticket_text, queue
        FROM resolved_tickets
        ORDER BY embedding <=> %s::vector
        LIMIT 5
    """, (ticket_embedding,))
    examples = cur.fetchall()
    conn.close()

    shots = "\n\n".join(
        f"<ticket>{t}</ticket>\n<queue>{q}</queue>"
        for t, q in examples
    )
    return (
        "Route each support ticket to the correct internal queue.\n\n"
        f"{shots}\n\n"
        f"<ticket>{ticket_text}</ticket>\n<queue>"
    )

If the system instruction says "always return ISO-8601 dates" but the examples show short-form dates like "Jan 5", the model receives contradictory signals. The examples usually win. Audit examples every time instructions change.

# Wrong: example contradicts instruction
System: "Return all dates as YYYY-MM-DD."

Example input:  "When did the order ship?"
Example output: "It shipped on Jan 5."   ← violates the instruction

# Right: example reinforces instruction
Example output: "It shipped on 2024-01-05."

Five examples of the same simple case (e.g., all positive sentiment) waste context and teach nothing about edge cases. The model already handles the easy case; examples should cover the decision boundaries it gets wrong.
Format mismatch between examples and live input

If examples show short single-sentence inputs but the live query is a multi-paragraph document, the model may compress the output format inappropriately. Match the style, length, and structure of live inputs in the examples.
Including chain-of-thought in examples when output must be terse

If the production system only uses the final answer line, including step-by-step reasoning traces in examples will cause the model to produce verbose outputs that break the downstream parser. Reserve chain-of-thought examples for tasks where the reasoning trace is part of the output contract.

Adding 20 examples when 4 would suffice pushes useful retrieval payloads or conversation history out of the context window. Measure accuracy gains per additional example and stop adding when the marginal gain drops below your quality bar.
— Select examples that cover distinct output patterns, not variations of the same pattern. Three diverse examples outperform ten similar ones.
Match example inputs to production distribution
— If live inputs are noisy, include noisy-input examples. If they are short-form queries, match that register.
— Pick XML tags, Markdown fences, or labeled fields and use them uniformly across every example and the live query.
— Treat example libraries like code: commit them to source control, log which version was active during each evaluation run.
A/B test changes on held-out eval data
— Replacing an example feels harmless but can shift accuracy by several percentage points. Measure before deploying.
— Static example sets are a starting point; for heterogeneous inputs, semantic retrieval of similar solved cases dramatically outperforms fixed sets.
Audit examples after schema or product changes
— Stale examples are silent regressions. Add example validation to the CI pipeline to catch format drift.
Validate output format in code, not just prompts
— Use JSON Schema validation or regex checks on the model output regardless of how many examples you provide.

Few-shot examples increase prompt token count linearly. If each example averages 150 tokens and the API charges per input token, adding four examples adds 600 tokens per call. At 10,000 calls/day that is 6 million additional input tokens daily — a material cost at commercial pricing.

(available on Anthropic and OpenAI APIs) amortises this cost: the static portion of the prompt (system instruction + examples) is cached after the first call and subsequent calls pay only for the cache hit rate at a fraction of the full cost. Structure prompts so examples appear before the dynamic live query to maximize the cacheable prefix length.
For latency, the dominant overhead in dynamic few-shot is the embedding + retrieval step. In practice:
: 2–10 ms for short texts with a small local model; 15–50 ms for a remote embedding API call.
: sub-millisecond for HNSW indices up to ~1M vectors; add 5–20 ms for a remote pgvector call.
: 20–60 ms end-to-end for dynamic retrieval, dwarfed by LLM generation time (200–2000 ms).
Accuracy saturates quickly with example count. The empirical rule of diminishing returns:
is a task-specific constant. For most classification and extraction tasks, peak accuracy is reached at 4–8 examples for large models (70B+ parameters) and at 8–16 for smaller models.
— declarative few-shot template builder with optional example selector integration.
— semantic example retrieval over an index, composable with any LLM call.
— visual pipeline editor with first-class support for few-shot prompt nodes.

# LangChain FewShotPromptTemplate — minimal example
from langchain.prompts import FewShotPromptTemplate, PromptTemplate

example_prompt = PromptTemplate(
    input_variables=["input", "output"],
    template="Input: {input}\nOutput: {output}",
)

few_shot = FewShotPromptTemplate(
    examples=[
        {"input": "happy", "output": "Positive"},
        {"input": "angry", "output": "Negative"},
        {"input": "meh",   "output": "Neutral"},
    ],
    example_prompt=example_prompt,
    suffix="Input: {adjective}\nOutput:",
    input_variables=["adjective"],
)

print(few_shot.format(adjective="thrilled"))
— PostgreSQL extension; ideal when examples already live in a relational database.
— hosted or self-hosted purpose-built vector stores; suitable for large example libraries (>100K entries).
— in-process, no network hop; good for moderate-scale example sets in memory.
— lightweight embedded vector store, zero-config for prototyping.
— evaluation platform with built-in few-shot experiment tracking.
— academic benchmarks measuring few-shot performance across task types.
— RAG + retrieval evaluation; its retrieval quality metrics apply directly to dynamic few-shot example selection.

Few-shot prompting occupies the intersection of low setup cost and meaningful format control. It is most often the right tool when zero-shot fails and fine-tuning is not justified by the volume or stability of the task.
If the live input is user-supplied and not sanitised, an adversary can inject text that breaks the example delimiter or overrides the pattern. For example, a user input of
could fool a naive XML-delimited prompt into treating the injected output as an authoritative example. Mitigations:
Escape or strip delimiter tokens from all user inputs before inserting them into the prompt.
Use a system prompt with the examples (never in the user turn for sensitive tasks).
Validate model output against a strict schema regardless of prompt construction.

Examples encode the biases of whoever collected them. An example library built from early-stage product data may encode stereotypes or incorrect business logic. Institute a review process: at minimum, have a domain expert audit every example before adding it to the production library.

When the live input distribution drifts from the example distribution (new product lines, seasonal vocabulary changes, language code-switching), static example sets degrade silently. Monitor output format compliance and semantic quality scores continuously. Set automated alerts when format compliance drops below a threshold.

Examples embedded in prompts are visible to anyone who can access the API call (logs, proxies, debugging tools). Never place PII, credentials, proprietary data, or personally identifiable customer information in few-shot examples. Use synthetic or anonymised data in the example library.
Few-shot prompting conditions a model's output via in-context learning — input-output demonstrations in the prompt, with no weight updates.
Quality and diversity of examples matter far more than quantity; 3–5 well-chosen examples outperform 20 near-duplicates.
Use static few-shot for known, stable tasks; switch to dynamic retrieval when inputs are heterogeneous or the example library grows beyond a handful.
Examples consume context tokens — account for this in latency, cost, and context-budget planning, and exploit prompt caching to amortise the cost.
Examples and written instructions must be consistent; when they conflict, examples typically dominate the model's behaviour.
Sanitise all user inputs before embedding them in few-shot prompts to prevent delimiter injection attacks.
Version, evaluate, and audit example sets on the same cadence as code — stale examples are silent regressions.
Few-shot sits between zero-shot and fine-tuning on the cost-quality curve; reach for it when zero-shot accuracy plateaus and training infrastructure is not yet justified.
